g.data-narration: Welcome to Directory Services - the backbone of enterprise identity management and network resource organization.
A directory service is a specialized database optimized for storing and retrieving information about network objects like users and printers.
g.text: A specialized database system that stores
information about network objects in a

g.data-narration: Directory services serve four main purposes: centralized identity management, resource discovery, authentication, and name resolution.
X.500 is the international standard for directory services, defining the DIB, DIT, DAP protocol, and schema framework.
LDAP is the lightweight protocol for accessing directory services, simpler than X.500's DAP, operating over TCP/IP with powerful search operations.
The Directory Information Tree organizes entries hierarchically with root, organizations, OUs, and leaf nodes representing objects.
A Distinguished Name uniquely identifies entries with full path, while RDN is the relative name at each hierarchy level.
g.text: Full path from leaf to root

g.data-narration: Object classes define categories of objects, specifying mandatory and optional attributes that entries can contain according to the schema.
Microsoft Active Directory is the most prevalent enterprise directory, extending LDAP with domain services, certificates, and federation.
g.text: Most prevalent enterprise directory service implementation

g.data-narration: Directory services are read-optimized hierarchical databases, while relational databases are designed for complex transactions with tabular data.
LDAP supports seven operations: Bind, Search, Add, Delete, Modify, Compare, and Unbind for directory management.
An LDAP search specifies base DN, scope, filter, and attributes to retrieve matching entries from the directory hierarchy.
User authentication involves binding with credentials, retrieving the user entry, comparing password hashes, and returning the result.

g.text: Client sends Bind request with DN

g.data-narration: Directory services use replication strategies like master-slave and multi-master to ensure high availability and performance across locations.
g.text: One master for updates, multiple slaves for reads
Multiple servers accept updates, sync changes
Fault tolerance | Load distribution | Geographic performance

g.data-narration: In summary, directory services provide hierarchical organization, LDAP enables standardized access, and they're essential for enterprise identity management.

g.text: Directory services are read-optimized hierarchical databases
LDAP is the standard protocol for directory access
DIT organizes entries hierarchically with DN as unique identifier
Active Directory is Microsoft's enterprise implementation
